chapters.title: Emotions are information we can process
chapters.text:
The emotions we feel are reactions to things happening around us or inside us. If your child breaks your laptop screen on the morning of an important presentation, you will probably feel angry. That anger is your brain's natural response to the consequences of what just happened.

Emotions are how your body interprets events.

Psychologists sort emotions into two kinds, integral and incidental. Integral emotions are tied to what is happening right now. The anger from the broken screen is a good example.

Incidental emotions are not tied to the current moment. Imagine you storm out of the house after the screen breaks, hoping to make it to the office early and figure out how to give your presentation. The stress from the morning shapes how you drive to work and how you speak with your coworkers when you get there. That is incidental emotion. You are being pushed by feelings that came from a different situation.

We move through both kinds of emotion every day. Integral emotions are easy to read in others. Incidental emotions can be confusing at first. The barista handing you coffee might seem rude for no reason, when really he had a fight with his wife before his shift. Spotting incidental emotions in others helps you give people the benefit of the doubt when they seem unfriendly.

Emotions are part of life for good. They are not bad by themselves, but the way we handle them shapes our well-being. Some people try to avoid the trouble that comes with mishandling emotions by refusing to feel them. That strategy does not work. You cannot suppress emotions for long. They will eventually burst, and the release is rarely pleasant.

Marc Brackett has written that if we do not express our emotions, they pile up like a debt that will eventually come due.

This summary is not about suppressing feelings. It is about understanding them, expressing them, and helping others do the same. The rest of the summary covers five skills for handling emotions well. They are recognize, understand, label, express, and regulate. You can remember them with the word RULER.

chapters.title: Get serious about recognizing emotions
chapters.text:
The first step in handling emotions is recognizing them. You cannot work with something you have not identified.

It sounds simple, but you would be surprised how many people never pause to notice what they are feeling in the moment. Try a quick exercise.

Pause now and try to recognize your current emotional state. Do not think about it. Just feel it. Notice how you feel right now. You may want to close your eyes and take a few slow breaths to focus.

How do you feel?

Can you put a word to it? It is fine if you cannot yet. At this stage, the goal is just to notice the feeling. We will get to labeling later.

Practice the exercise a few times a day to build the habit. The more you do it, the sharper you get.

Recognizing what you feel is the critical first step in emotional intelligence.

Once you can spot your own emotions, how do you spot them in others? What helps you read what someone else is feeling?

Unless you can read minds, you will need to lean on your intuition. Few of us can read minds, so intuition is the next best option. The problem is that your intuition is rarely accurate with people you do not know well. With that in mind, here are three things you can watch for to recognize what someone else is feeling quickly. Their facial expression, their tone of voice, and their body language.

Start practicing today. Use those three signals to guess how someone is feeling. Once you have a guess, check in with them. See if you are right or close. It is fine if you are wrong. Keep practicing until you get better.

Recognition is just the first step. Let us look at the others.

chapters.title: Work to understand what is behind the emotions you feel
chapters.text:
Every emotion you have felt was triggered by something or someone. Even though there are common themes across human emotions, each person's feelings are still their own.

What scares one person can excite another, so broad generalizations do not help. Imagine Mr. A, who is terrified of public speaking. He would rather do anything than stand in front of a crowd. Now imagine Miss B, a professional speaker, who lights up when she shares ideas with a large audience. Two different people, the same situation, totally different feelings.

Because everyone is different, be careful with broad labels when judging emotions.

The key to reading your own emotions or someone else's is to ask why.

Picture a conversation with your child after work. At some point, he says he hates Jack, your neighbor's son, and never wants to play with him again.

How do you figure out what your child is feeling and why? It is straightforward. You ask questions. You might ask why he hates Jack, what Jack did, and so on. One question often leads to the next until you reach the real feeling behind the statement.

Understanding matters because, without knowing the cause, it is hard to find a fix.

Use this back-and-forth approach for your own emotions as well as for the people around you.

chapters.title: Label your emotions accurately
chapters.text:
Take a moment to think about how many words you use to describe emotions on a regular basis. If you are like most people, the count is probably under 10.

One study suggests that people use only 6 to 7 English words to describe how they feel. Given that the English language has more than 2,000 emotion words, the gap is hard to ignore.

The fact that we use so few words for feelings says a lot about how emotions are treated. It is clear that emotions do not get the attention they deserve. In large part, this is because most people are never taught about emotions in school. Some people do not learn emotional intelligence in any formal setting until graduate school. The good news is that organizations like the Yale Center for Emotional Intelligence are stepping in to train educators and students in the basics of emotional maturity. There is still much work to be done.

The danger of a small emotion vocabulary is that you risk mislabeling what you feel. That has two effects. You will not read the feeling correctly, so you will miss the chance to address it. Other people also cannot help, because you have not told them what is actually going on.

Naming an emotion well raises the odds that you will handle it well.

It is clearly worth improving your emotion vocabulary. Here are two ideas to consider. First, pay attention to emotion words as you move through your day. If you stay alert, you will spot words you already know but do not use often. Make a point of using them more often until they feel natural.

Second, download the Mood Meter app, designed by Marc Brackett and his team with HopeLab. The app includes a wide range of emotion words and lets you record, track, and understand your changing emotional states. You can find it at moodmeterapp.com.

One small thing worth knowing is that kvell is a Yiddish word for the overwhelming love and pride you feel when you see what your child can do. There is no single English word that captures that exact feeling.

chapters.title: Learn to express your emotions
chapters.text:
As we covered earlier, emotions are reactions to what is happening inside or outside us. That purpose means emotions are not meant to be suppressed.

If your spouse is doing something that bothers you, emotions like anger and resentment will show up. Those emotions are not there to be buried. They are signals that something in your marriage needs attention. Until you deal with the underlying cause, the anger and resentment will only grow.

This rule also applies to positive feelings. They need an outlet too.

Naturally, we want to share our emotions with the people around us, especially the ones we love. The fear of rejection or the worry that we are making others uncomfortable often stops us from speaking up.

If you find yourself in that position, it still helps to have one or two people you can trust with your feelings. That could be a friend, a partner, or a mentor. Strong emotions are not meant to be carried alone, and the people around you cannot help if you never share what you are going through.

Do not keep intense feelings to yourself, pleasant or not. Sharing them with people you trust is a healthy choice.

It is true that some feelings are not safe to share with certain people. When that happens, what is the alternative? A proven outlet is writing. The power of pen and paper should not be overlooked. When you feel there is no one who will listen the way your emotions deserve without making things worse, pick up a pen, open a notebook, or use a digital notepad, and let the words out. It works.

chapters.title: The heart of emotional intelligence is the ability to regulate emotions
chapters.text:
Emotion regulation is the last skill you need to become what some call an emotion scientist. The other four skills covered in this summary matter, but without the ability to regulate your own emotions and help others do the same, they go nowhere. This chapter covers four strategies for regulating emotions.

Mindful breathing

The brain responds to strong emotions by activating the sympathetic nervous system. That is why you feel tension, a racing heart, and sweaty palms when emotions spike. Mindful breathing can calm you down quickly. The technique is to pay attention to how you are breathing. Watch your breath the way you would watch someone else's, then breathe in and out on purpose. That simple act shifts your attention away from the strong emotion and its cause.

Forward-thinking strategies

These help you get ahead of situations before they happen. Forward-thinking is about predicting what is coming and choosing how you will respond. Suppose your boss always flares up during board meetings. He has done it for as long as you have known him, so the pattern is clear. Forward-thinking means expecting him to flare up in the next meeting and deciding in advance how you will keep your cool.

Attention shifting strategies

The core idea is that moving your attention away from an emotion reduces its grip. There are many ways to shift attention, and you can get creative once you understand the principle.

Cognitive reframing

You have probably heard the line that nothing is good or bad, but thinking makes it so. The line comes from Hamlet, though many people have said something similar.

William Shakespeare wrote that there is nothing either good or bad, but thinking makes it so.

The point is that our interpretations matter more than the events we live through. Apply this idea to emotion regulation by looking for the good even in the worst situations. It will help you respond in a more useful way.

What you think about what happens to you matters more than what actually happens.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Emotions matter for our well-being and for the quality of our relationships. The emotions we feel can be pleasant or unpleasant, high energy or low energy, but none of them are bad in themselves. When we learn to manage them well, we give ourselves the chance to experience life fully. Emotional intelligence as a skill also helps us support others with their feelings.

This summary covered the RULER skills needed to become an emotion scientist. Here is a short recap. The letter R stands for recognize your own emotions and those of others. The letter U stands for understand the emotions you have identified. Find their source and try to learn why you or the other person is feeling them.

The letter L stands for label emotions correctly. Build your emotion vocabulary so you do not confuse one feeling for another. The letter E stands for express your feelings in ways that fit the norms of your culture, so others can respond with empathy. The letter R stands for regulate your emotions, so your emotions do not regulate you.

Start using the RULER skills today. The more you put the strategies into practice, the better you will get over time.

A practical step is this. Have you been holding back feelings about something? Now is the time to express them so you can enjoy real mental freedom. Talk to someone close to you about it, or find a person you trust with your feelings. If neither is an option, you can release what you have been carrying by writing it down.
